texts.label: text
texts.orig: Energy storage using Li-based rechargeable batteries is essential for the uptake of renewable energy. This mini-review highlights the challenge of extracting lithium from Chinese salt-lake brine. The paper also reviews current and emerging technologies that can be used to extract lithium from a high Mg/Li ratio brine which is typical of most Chinese salt lakes.
texts.text: Energy storage using Li-based rechargeable batteries is essential for the uptake of renewable energy. This mini-review highlights the challenge of extracting lithium from Chinese salt-lake brine. The paper also reviews current and emerging technologies that can be used to extract lithium from a high Mg/Li ratio brine which is typical of most Chinese salt lakes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium, the lightest metallic element, is one of the most important commodities because of its wide applications in nuclear fusion, chemical and metallurgical industries, 1 and particularly in rechargeable lithium ion batteries. 2 The rapid expansion of the electric vehicles 3 and grid energy storage 4 markets places a strong demand for lithium from the battery industry. The demand for lithium has dramatically increased in recent years. 5,6 The price of lithium carbonate tripled from 2015 up to 129 000 RMB/t in December 2016. 7
texts.text: Lithium, the lightest metallic element, is one of the most important commodities because of its wide applications in nuclear fusion, chemical and metallurgical industries, 1 and particularly in rechargeable lithium ion batteries. 2 The rapid expansion of the electric vehicles 3 and grid energy storage 4 markets places a strong demand for lithium from the battery industry. The demand for lithium has dramatically increased in recent years. 5,6 The price of lithium carbonate tripled from 2015 up to 129 000 RMB/t in December 2016. 7

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium can be obtained from seawater, Li-containing ores, and lithium-rich salt-lake brines. Despite the large lithium reserves in the ocean of about 231.4 trillion tonnes, lithium recovery from seawater is not yet economically viable because of the low concentration in seawater of around 0.178 mg L -1 . 8 There are other major lithium sources: namely Licontaining ores ( e.g. spodumene, petalite and lepidolite) and salt-lake brines. The latter account for over 80% of total recoverable lithium deposits. 9 There is a clear trend for the lithium industry to shift from ores to salt-lake brines 10 as the lithium-rich ore reserve is diminished and cost-effective tech-

The latter account for over 80% of total recoverable lithium deposits. 9 There is a clear trend for the lithium industry to shift from ores to salt-lake brines 10 as the lithium-rich ore reserve is diminished and cost-effective tech-

texts.text: nologies for lithium production from salt-lake brines start to emerge. 3 Lithium concentration in salt-lake brines varies from site to site; however, in most cases, it is much higher than that in seawater, as demonstrated in Table 1 which summarises lithium concentrations in several Chinese salt lakes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Chinese salt-lake brine
texts.text: Chinese salt-lake brine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In China, lithium-rich salt lakes are located mostly in the Qinghai Tibet Plateau. This region is known for its significant lithium resources. Lithium deposits in the Qinghai provinces were estimated to be about 244.7 million tonnes. 11 The enriched source of lithium in those brines is related to geothermal activity from volcanic systems 12 and anatectic magmatism. 13 The volcanic hot water from the area between the middle and southern Kunlun faults was an important source of potassium, boron, and lithium in the Qarhan salt lake. Based on the location of the brine in the salt lake, natural brine can be classified into surface, intergranular, and over-saturated brine. 13 The last two types can be used for mineral extraction and further classified into original brine, brine after precipitation of sodium salt, brine after crystallization of potassium salt, and concentrated brine (Fig. 1). As an example, for the East Taijinar salt lake, the concentration of lithium increased sequentially to reach 4 -5 g L -1 in the concentrated brine (Fig. 1).
texts.text: In China, lithium-rich salt lakes are located mostly in the Qinghai Tibet Plateau. This region is known for its significant lithium resources. Lithium deposits in the Qinghai provinces were estimated to be about 244.7 million tonnes. 11 The enriched source of lithium in those brines is related to geothermal activity from volcanic systems 12 and anatectic magmatism. 13 The volcanic hot water from the area between the middle and southern Kunlun faults was an important source of potassium, boron, and lithium in the Qarhan salt lake. Based on the location of the brine in the salt lake, natural brine can be classified into surface, intergranular, and over-saturated brine. 13 The last two types can be used for mineral extraction and further classified into original brine, brine after precipitation of sodium salt, brine after crystallization of potassium salt, and concentrated brine (Fig. 1). As an example, for the East Taijinar salt lake, the concentration of lithium increased sequentially to reach 4 -5 g L -1 in the concentrated brine (Fig. 1).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Lithium extraction from Chinese salt-lake brines: opportunities, challenges, and future outlook
texts.text: Lithium extraction from Chinese salt-lake brines: opportunities, challenges, and future outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jian Feng Song, ab Long D. Nghiem, c Xue-Mei Li a and Tao He
texts.text: Jian Feng Song, ab Long D. Nghiem, c Xue-Mei Li a and Tao He

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chinese salt-lake brine is mainly of the magnesium sulfate subtype with a high Mg/Li ratio. Mining lithium from Chinese salt-lake brine has been a decades-long technical challenge. The pros and cons of various technologies are briefly discussed. Chemical extraction has been the most important technology for the recovery of lithium from Chinese salt-lake brine with a high Mg/Li ratio. Several other innovative technologies, including lithium ion sieves, membrane separation, and electro -electrodialysis, have also emerged as potential options.
texts.text: Chinese salt-lake brine is mainly of the magnesium sulfate subtype with a high Mg/Li ratio. Mining lithium from Chinese salt-lake brine has been a decades-long technical challenge. The pros and cons of various technologies are briefly discussed. Chemical extraction has been the most important technology for the recovery of lithium from Chinese salt-lake brine with a high Mg/Li ratio. Several other innovative technologies, including lithium ion sieves, membrane separation, and electro -electrodialysis, have also emerged as potential options.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: magnesium sulfate and sodium sulfate subtypes), and carbonate type. 23 Carbonate-rich lakes are located in the southern and south western part of the plateau, and magnesium/ sulfate-rich lakes are in the Qaidam Basin, in the northern part of the plateau. 13
texts.text: magnesium sulfate and sodium sulfate subtypes), and carbonate type. 23 Carbonate-rich lakes are located in the southern and south western part of the plateau, and magnesium/ sulfate-rich lakes are in the Qaidam Basin, in the northern part of the plateau. 13

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most important lithium-bearing deposit in the zone of carbonate-type lakes is Zabuye Lake. Because of very low magnesium concentrations (Zabuye and Jezecaka Lake in Table 1), production of lithium from these lakes can be readily achieved. Lithium carbonate can be precipitated directly from the brine by evaporation. This is similar to the process currently used to extract lithium from the Silver Peak Lake in the US and Atacama Lake in Chile. 24 The Mg/Li ratios in brine from Silver Peak Lake and Atacama Lake are only 2 and 0.1 -1, respectively.
texts.text: The most important lithium-bearing deposit in the zone of carbonate-type lakes is Zabuye Lake. Because of very low magnesium concentrations (Zabuye and Jezecaka Lake in Table 1), production of lithium from these lakes can be readily achieved. Lithium carbonate can be precipitated directly from the brine by evaporation. This is similar to the process currently used to extract lithium from the Silver Peak Lake in the US and Atacama Lake in Chile. 24 The Mg/Li ratios in brine from Silver Peak Lake and Atacama Lake are only 2 and 0.1 -1, respectively.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 1 The sequential evaporation pond for the enrichment of lithium from brine at East Taijinar salt lake. The intergranular brine was pumped from an underground basin to a trench (A); brine was further distributed via a reservoir (B); evaporation by solar power to precipitate sodium chloride (C); the brine after production of potassium chloride, at this stage the lithium concentration was about 2 g L -1 (D); further evaporation of the brine enriched the lithium concentration up to 4 -5 g L -1 (E); finally, the concentrated brine was used for the lithium recovery. Lithium concentration was measured in our lab.
texts.text: Fig. 1 The sequential evaporation pond for the enrichment of lithium from brine at East Taijinar salt lake. The intergranular brine was pumped from an underground basin to a trench (A); brine was further distributed via a reservoir (B); evaporation by solar power to precipitate sodium chloride (C); the brine after production of potassium chloride, at this stage the lithium concentration was about 2 g L -1 (D); further evaporation of the brine enriched the lithium concentration up to 4 -5 g L -1 (E); finally, the concentrated brine was used for the lithium recovery. Lithium concentration was measured in our lab.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Lithium recovery from brine of high Mg/Li ratio
texts.text: Lithium recovery from brine of high Mg/Li ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most salt-lake brines in China are of the magnesium sulfate subtype and the ratio of Mg/Li can be as high as 500 (Table 1). 16,18 The chemical precipitation approach that has been successfully applied to low calcium and magnesium brines (such as those from Zabuye and Jezecaka Lake) would consume a large quantity of chemicals and generate a huge amount of solid waste. 25 In addition, lithium loss due to co-precipitation and adsorption to calcium/magnesium precipitate is also significant.
texts.text: Most salt-lake brines in China are of the magnesium sulfate subtype and the ratio of Mg/Li can be as high as 500 (Table 1). 16,18 The chemical precipitation approach that has been successfully applied to low calcium and magnesium brines (such as those from Zabuye and Jezecaka Lake) would consume a large quantity of chemicals and generate a huge amount of solid waste. 25 In addition, lithium loss due to co-precipitation and adsorption to calcium/magnesium precipitate is also significant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The technologies to extract lithium from brine with a high Mg/Li ratio include calcination, adsorption, extraction and membrane separation 25,30 (Table 2). These technologies have been explored for potential large-scale production. However, most of these technologies are still at a pilot stage or in small-scale production. A project for 10 000 t per year Li2CO3 was implemented using calcination technology at East Taijinar salt lake, 31 but has not yet reached the target operation due to the high energy cost and emission of acid mist which corrodes the equipment and causes severe air pollution. Adsorption using lithium ion selective sieve was claimed to reach commercialisation (capacity of 10 000 t per year) in 2007. However, until now, the project has not yet achieved full-scale operation due to several undisclosed technical issues. Nanofiltration and electrodialysis membranes have also been investigated for lithium recovery from salt-lake brine over the last decade. The separation of magnesium and lithium by either nanofiltration or electrodialysis is technically challenging given their very similar hydrated radius. 29 As a traditional technology, chemical extraction has seen a recent resurgence in both research and industry. In the following section, technologies with a strong potential will be further discussed.
texts.text: The technologies to extract lithium from brine with a high Mg/Li ratio include calcination, adsorption, extraction and membrane separation 25,30 (Table 2). These technologies have been explored for potential large-scale production. However, most of these technologies are still at a pilot stage or in small-scale production. A project for 10 000 t per year Li2CO3 was implemented using calcination technology at East Taijinar salt lake, 31 but has not yet reached the target operation due to the high energy cost and emission of acid mist which corrodes the equipment and causes severe air pollution. Adsorption using lithium ion selective sieve was claimed to reach commercialisation (capacity of 10 000 t per year) in 2007. However, until now, the project has not yet achieved full-scale operation due to several undisclosed technical issues. Nanofiltration and electrodialysis membranes have also been investigated for lithium recovery from salt-lake brine over the last decade. The separation of magnesium and lithium by either nanofiltration or electrodialysis is technically challenging given their very similar hydrated radius. 29 As a traditional technology, chemical extraction has seen a recent resurgence in both research and industry. In the following section, technologies with a strong potential will be further discussed.

These oxides contain a series of chemicals, such as the spinel manganese oxides, 32 -35 and nanostructure MnO2. 36 Inspired by the lithium ion sieve, titanium lithium ionic and lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) sieves have also been investigated. 37 -40 These absorbents have been tested for recovery of lithium from brine from Qarhan saline lake. 41 Challenges for ionic sieves include: (1) dissolution of metal ion from the adsorbent together with lithium ions during the acid treatment; (2) splitting of sieve particles into smaller ones; (3) collecting the particles, and the washing and regenerating processes are still expensive; (4) reduced adsorbing capacity due to blockage of the ion channels. Hence, to im-

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Chemical extraction
texts.text: Chemical extraction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Liquid -liquid extraction has been widely studied for recovering lithium from brine with a high ratio of Mg/Li. β -diketones and n -butanol were reported as extractants to extract lithium from brine in the 1970s. 42,43 In addition to these studies, neutral organophosphorus extractants 44,45 have also been investigated. One typical extraction system is tributyl phosphate (TBP)/kerosene-FeCl3. In this system, FeCl3 solution plays the role of a co-extracting agent, which is crucial for extracting lithium. In the mechanism of extraction by TBP, 46 -48 the co-extraction performances of methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK), 49 N , N -bis IJ 2-ethylhexyl) acetamide (N523), 50 and ionic liquid 51 were investigated. As to other extractants, N503, 52 N523, 53 di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric acid (D2EHPA) were studied as single extractants. Of those extractants, TBP is probably the most suitable for brine with a high Mg/Li ratio and a pilot-scale extraction process based on this extractant has been studied.
texts.text: Liquid -liquid extraction has been widely studied for recovering lithium from brine with a high ratio of Mg/Li. β -diketones and n -butanol were reported as extractants to extract lithium from brine in the 1970s. 42,43 In addition to these studies, neutral organophosphorus extractants 44,45 have also been investigated. One typical extraction system is tributyl phosphate (TBP)/kerosene-FeCl3. In this system, FeCl3 solution plays the role of a co-extracting agent, which is crucial for extracting lithium. In the mechanism of extraction by TBP, 46 -48 the co-extraction performances of methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK), 49 N , N -bis IJ 2-ethylhexyl) acetamide (N523), 50 and ionic liquid 51 were investigated. As to other extractants, N503, 52 N523, 53 di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric acid (D2EHPA) were studied as single extractants. Of those extractants, TBP is probably the most suitable for brine with a high Mg/Li ratio and a pilot-scale extraction process based on this extractant has been studied.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Equipment selection has been a major challenge in implementing chemical extraction for lithium recovery. A mix-settler was selected as the extraction equipment by Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes of Chinese Academy of Sciences in the 1990s. Some plants have also used a mix-settler for pilotscale production. A typical example is the mix-settler

texts.label: text
texts.orig: equipment built to recover lithium from Da Qaidam salt lake in 2016. However, its large footprint, large liquid volume, severe corrosion by the extractant and long equilibrium time are still among a few remaining technical issues to be solved. The extractant and extraction processes have been optimised; 49,50 however, selection of suitable equipment is still a technical and scientific challenge.
texts.text: equipment built to recover lithium from Da Qaidam salt lake in 2016. However, its large footprint, large liquid volume, severe corrosion by the extractant and long equilibrium time are still among a few remaining technical issues to be solved. The extractant and extraction processes have been optimised; 49,50 however, selection of suitable equipment is still a technical and scientific challenge.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To reduce the large liquid volume and long equilibrium time, a centrifuge system was proposed in a key project from the Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2014. Since a large quantity of fluid (organic extractant and brine) is involved in extraction, exceptionally large anticorrosive centrifuges were needed; furthermore, the low energy efficiency of the centrifuge leads to high energy cost. As a partner in this project, the membrane team from Shanghai Advanced Research Institute (SARI) selected different approaches: membrane chemical extraction and reciprocal column (Karr column) extraction.
texts.text: To reduce the large liquid volume and long equilibrium time, a centrifuge system was proposed in a key project from the Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2014. Since a large quantity of fluid (organic extractant and brine) is involved in extraction, exceptionally large anticorrosive centrifuges were needed; furthermore, the low energy efficiency of the centrifuge leads to high energy cost. As a partner in this project, the membrane team from Shanghai Advanced Research Institute (SARI) selected different approaches: membrane chemical extraction and reciprocal column (Karr column) extraction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The potential advantages of membrane extraction techniques are low capital and operating costs, low energy consumption, potentially small footprint (compared to a mix-settler). In a membrane extraction process, a membrane barrier, which is permeable to cations ( i.e. Li + , Mg 2+ , Na + ) and impermeable to organic extraction, is located at the interface between the organic extractant and brine; lithium ions are selectively extracted and purified. However, a solvent-stable membrane barrier is required with long-term stability. 54 -56 A recent report on a solvent-stable hydrophilic nanoporous poly(ethylene-co-vinyl alcohol) membrane 15 showed stable lithium extraction for 1037 hours. This stable performance indicates the potential of the present membrane for large-scale applications.
texts.text: The potential advantages of membrane extraction techniques are low capital and operating costs, low energy consumption, potentially small footprint (compared to a mix-settler). In a membrane extraction process, a membrane barrier, which is permeable to cations ( i.e. Li + , Mg 2+ , Na + ) and impermeable to organic extraction, is located at the interface between the organic extractant and brine; lithium ions are selectively extracted and purified. However, a solvent-stable membrane barrier is required with long-term stability. 54 -56 A recent report on a solvent-stable hydrophilic nanoporous poly(ethylene-co-vinyl alcohol) membrane 15 showed stable lithium extraction for 1037 hours. This stable performance indicates the potential of the present membrane for large-scale applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reciprocal extraction columns have been widely used in the petrochemical industry. The Karr reciprocating plate extraction column with high load capacity is an effective solution. This equipment is efficient in treating a large amount of liquid, with a small footprint, easy automation, and tolerance to liquid with a high load of foulant. After thorough analysis and balance of the treatment capacity, energy consumption as well as the risk of separation of organics from brine, we have evaluated column technology for lithium extraction. A high load Karr column was developed to extract lithium from West Taijinar lake brine in our research; the TBP system was selected and a new chemical exchange process was used to improve the purity of the lithium product. The purity of lithium can be controlled and the highest value was 99.9% (Fig. 2).
texts.text: Reciprocal extraction columns have been widely used in the petrochemical industry. The Karr reciprocating plate extraction column with high load capacity is an effective solution. This equipment is efficient in treating a large amount of liquid, with a small footprint, easy automation, and tolerance to liquid with a high load of foulant. After thorough analysis and balance of the treatment capacity, energy consumption as well as the risk of separation of organics from brine, we have evaluated column technology for lithium extraction. A high load Karr column was developed to extract lithium from West Taijinar lake brine in our research; the TBP system was selected and a new chemical exchange process was used to improve the purity of the lithium product. The purity of lithium can be controlled and the highest value was 99.9% (Fig. 2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Electro chemical process
texts.text: Electro chemical process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electro -electrodialysis with a bipolar membrane (EEDBM) has been investigated as a new method to extract lithium from brines. 57 Discarded rechargeable batteries have been used recently for lithium recovery. The lithium ion capturing electrode behaves as a lithium ion sieve; the process is driven by electricity. Lithium ions are inserted into FePO4 from lithium salt solution during the discharge reaction of a lithium
texts.text: Electro -electrodialysis with a bipolar membrane (EEDBM) has been investigated as a new method to extract lithium from brines. 57 Discarded rechargeable batteries have been used recently for lithium recovery. The lithium ion capturing electrode behaves as a lithium ion sieve; the process is driven by electricity. Lithium ions are inserted into FePO4 from lithium salt solution during the discharge reaction of a lithium

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ion phosphate rechargeable battery. Based on this reaction, a battery technology that consists of a lithium capturing electrode, the FePO4 anode, and LiFePO4 cathode was studied. 58,59 A chloride-capturing electrode (Ag) 60,61 and sodium thiosulfate were found to have an optimum redox potential 62 during the process of lithium recovery. Another ion sieve obtained from spinel phases of lithium manganese oxides (LiMO), such as LiMn2O4 and Li1.33Mn1.67O4, retains the framework of the parent compounds and it is highly selective for lithium in aqueous environments. 34,63 Spinel LiMn2O4 (LMO) has been reported as a lithium ion intercalation electrode and polypyrrole (PPy) reversible chloride electrode. 64 A λ -MnO2 positive electrode and an Ag negative electrode 65 were also investigated. To reduce the cost and increase long-term stability, a λ -MnO2/activated carbon hybrid supercapacitor system was studied to recovery lithium from solution. 66 However, the redox reactions caused the dissolution of manganese ions and destablized the MnO2. Therefore, the development of new materials for lithium-ion-capturing electrodes remains an active subject for further study.
texts.text: ion phosphate rechargeable battery. Based on this reaction, a battery technology that consists of a lithium capturing electrode, the FePO4 anode, and LiFePO4 cathode was studied. 58,59 A chloride-capturing electrode (Ag) 60,61 and sodium thiosulfate were found to have an optimum redox potential 62 during the process of lithium recovery. Another ion sieve obtained from spinel phases of lithium manganese oxides (LiMO), such as LiMn2O4 and Li1.33Mn1.67O4, retains the framework of the parent compounds and it is highly selective for lithium in aqueous environments. 34,63 Spinel LiMn2O4 (LMO) has been reported as a lithium ion intercalation electrode and polypyrrole (PPy) reversible chloride electrode. 64 A λ -MnO2 positive electrode and an Ag negative electrode 65 were also investigated. To reduce the cost and increase long-term stability, a λ -MnO2/activated carbon hybrid supercapacitor system was studied to recovery lithium from solution. 66 However, the redox reactions caused the dissolution of manganese ions and destablized the MnO2. Therefore, the development of new materials for lithium-ion-capturing electrodes remains an active subject for further study.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Summary and outlook
texts.text: Summary and outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Brine has been the most important target for lithium ion extraction for lithium battery development. Particularly in China, salt-lake brine, mainly of the sulfate type, has been the core for lithium recovery. However, the high Mg/Li ratio commonly found in most Chinese salt lakes is still a challenge for large-scale lithium production. Potential technologies to overcome this challenge include lithium sieves, chemical extraction, nanofiltration, and electro -electrodialysis. Chemical extraction is the most promising approach in the near future. We compared the pros and cons of current extraction techniques and equipment including mix-settler, centrifuge, column and membrane contactors. Column extraction technology was promising. Novel extraction technologies driven by electrochemical reactions were introduced; the development of new materials for electrodes and long-term stability, and selectivity are the main challenges for these potential technologies.
texts.text: Brine has been the most important target for lithium ion extraction for lithium battery development. Particularly in China, salt-lake brine, mainly of the sulfate type, has been the core for lithium recovery. However, the high Mg/Li ratio commonly found in most Chinese salt lakes is still a challenge for large-scale lithium production. Potential technologies to overcome this challenge include lithium sieves, chemical extraction, nanofiltration, and electro -electrodialysis. Chemical extraction is the most promising approach in the near future. We compared the pros and cons of current extraction techniques and equipment including mix-settler, centrifuge, column and membrane contactors. Column extraction technology was promising. Novel extraction technologies driven by electrochemical reactions were introduced; the development of new materials for electrodes and long-term stability, and selectivity are the main challenges for these potential technologies.

table_cells.text: • The brine is sprayed dry to get a solid mixture of MgCl 2 · 2H 2 O and LiCl. After carbonation to MgO and LiCl in a rotary kiln at 800 - 100 ° C, the LiCl is dissolved because the solubility of MgO is low; LiCl is then precipitated to Li 2 CO 3 . • 10000 t per year pilot in East Taijinar; but stopped. • Mature technology but high energy cost; • Air pollution due to emission of acid mist. 26

• Lithium ion sieve: lithium ion selective sieves can selectively absorb Li + from brine; then Li + are desorbed by dilute HCl solution to obtain a lithium-rich solution. • 10000 t per year in Qarhan installed in 2007; not yet in full-scale operation. • Low cost and easy to realize larger scale; • Dissolution of adsorbent; • Reduced adsorbing capacity due to blockage of the ion channels. 26

Organic extractants are able to selectively extract Li + from brine; theoretical basis is known; however, suitable extracting equipment with small footprint, low cost, high efficiency is required. 17 Mixer-settler 27 • 1000 t per year in Da Qaidam;

• Electrodialysis: Monovalent and divalent cations diffuse at different speeds in electric field across the ion exchange membrane; separation occurs for Li + and Mg 2+ . • Project of 10000 t per year in East Taijinar; not yet full-scale operation; • Nanofiltration (NF) membrane separates monovalent ions and divalent ions; thus is able to separate Mg 2+ from Li + ; the brine has to be diluted to reduce osmotic pressure; • Failed in pilot stage; NF process can separate the monovalent and divalent salts, and the same for Li + and Mg 2+ ; • Easy to control and low energy consumption; • Emerging technology; no commercial membrane system available; potential membrane fouling/scaling. 29
